beats.text: Welcome to Say... It . in French — the course where you don't just hear French, you speak it. Starting right now.
Every lesson follows four steps: Meet a word, Echo it back out loud, Build it into a sentence, Make It Yours by writing your own. Let's start.
Today's goal: greet someone in French. Let's meet your first word.
That "on" in the middle is nasal — let the air flow through your nose, not a hard N.
Hi — or bye. Casual, for friends, not your boss.
Quick check before we go on. Which one greets your boss?
And which one greets a friend?
Good instinct — same idea, different company.
A quick note on manners. In France, bonjour isn't optional — walk into a shop without saying it first, and even a simple question can come across as rude. Greet, then ask.
Once more — a little quicker this time.

segments.title: Segment 7 · RECALL — merci, s'il vous plaît

beats.text: Two more — say the English first.
Now flip it — English to French.
Listen for that "r" — it comes from the back of your throat, not the front of your mouth like an English r.
One more — really growl that r.
Big check-in. I'll say a word in French — say the English out loud before I move on.
Now the other way — I'll say the English, you say the French.
Now we build a sentence, one piece at a time. We start here.
Add "madame" — a polite word for a woman.
Now swap it — "monsieur" for a man.
One more piece — now it's a whole little exchange.

segments.title: Segment 16 · BUILD — Au revoir, madame. Merci !

beats.text: Now swap the first piece — arriving becomes leaving.
Before we add one more piece, you say a whole one. Arriving, to a woman, with thanks.
Now leaving, to a man, with thanks.
This is good evening — bonjour's after-dark twin.
And monsieur — sir, madame's counterpart, for a man. Say the whole thing.

One more note. The switch from bonjour to bonsoir isn't at a fixed hour — it's whenever it starts to feel like evening to the people around you. When in doubt, listen for which one they say first, and match it.
Let's mix everything you know into a few quick little scenes.
Four tiny scenes — all words you already own.
However you order them, they still work.
Twelve little scenes now, and counting.
Your turn. Morning or evening right now? That's your first word. Add madame or monsieur. Say it out loud, and write it down.
Good evening, ma'am. Thank you, goodbye! Give yourself a point for every piece you got right.

Two spelling notes for when you write these down. S'il vous plaît has an apostrophe right after the S. And à bientôt carries two accent marks — a small slanted line over that first à, and a little hat over the o in bientôt.
Let's try a different scene. This time you're leaving, not arriving. Say goodbye, thank them, and choose madame or monsieur.
Here's one way to say it.
Thank you, sir. Goodbye! However you said it, that's a real French sentence, built by you.
Excuse me — or sorry. For bumping into someone, or interrupting politely.
See you soon — for someone you'll actually see again.
These two are brand new — let's lock them in right away.
Now you say them. Excuse me, or sorry.
One more scene. You bump into someone by accident — apologize, then greet them.
Here's one way to say it.
Excuse me, ma'am! Hello. Three scenes now, three sentences — and none of them were mine.
One more kind of check. I'll describe a moment — you say the word before I do.
Someone holds the door open for you.
You step on someone's foot by accident.
You're leaving a shop, and you'll be back again tomorrow.
You want the waiter's attention, politely.
Four real situations, four right answers — and every one came from you, not the screen.
One last scene — nearly everything you learned today, back to back.
Pardon, monsieur ! Bonjour. S'il vous plaît... Merci ! Au revoir, à bientôt !
Excuse me, sir! Hello. Please... Thank you! Goodbye, see you soon! One little scene, seven of your words.
One more scene, this time starting in the evening.
Bonsoir, madame ! Pardon... Oui, s'il vous plaît. Merci ! À bientôt !
Good evening, ma'am! Excuse me... Yes, please. Thank you! See you soon! Same words, brand new scene.
Last scenario. This time someone greets you first — imagine hearing "Bonsoir, madame" or "Bonsoir, monsieur," whichever fits you. How do you reply, and how do you say goodbye when you leave?
Good evening! Thanks. See you soon! Four scenes now — arriving, leaving, apologizing, and replying. All four directions, and all four were yours.
Speed round — twelve words, fast. Just say the English.
All twelve. You didn't just hear French today — you spoke it, over and over.
Everything from today, back to back.
Bonjour. Salut. Bonsoir. Au revoir. Merci. S'il vous plaît. Oui. Non. Pardon. Madame. Monsieur. À bientôt.
Twelve words — and you said every one of them yourself.
Last check — no French on screen, no help. Just you.
Four for four. That's not luck — that's practice.
One last one, a bigger one. A whole sentence this time — "Good evening, ma'am. Thank you, goodbye."
You just produced a full French sentence from an English cue, with nothing on screen to lean on. That's the whole point of this course.
Quick scorecard for today. You met twelve words.
You built sentences, piece by piece, more than once.
And you wrote sentences of your own, completely from scratch, four separate times.
That's a full lesson, done the right way — one word at a time, out loud.
Twelve words in the bank — twelve out of two hundred and thirty-five for A1.
First lesson done. Next time: your name, and how you're doing. À bientôt.
